name: EXPLAIN ANALYZE and Index Optimization
description: Diagnose slow queries with EXPLAIN ANALYZE and create targeted indexes to dramatically improve PostgreSQL query performance.
trendingReason: Query performance tuning is the highest-impact skill for production database optimization
prerequisites: PostgreSQL database with tables and data

steps.title: Analyze a query execution plan
steps.description: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to see the actual execution plan with real timings.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 1001 AND status = 'pending';"
steps.tip: BUFFERS shows I/O statistics. Look for Seq Scan on large tables as the primary indicator of missing indexes.

steps.title: Find missing indexes with pg_stat_user_tables
steps.description: Identify tables with high sequential scan counts that could benefit from indexes.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "SELECT relname, seq_scan, seq_tup_read, idx_scan, n_live_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE seq_scan > 100 ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC LIMIT 10;"

steps.title: Create an index for a common query pattern
steps.description: Add a composite index to support filtered queries efficiently.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_status ON orders (customer_id, status);"
steps.tip: CONCURRENTLY builds the index without locking the table for writes. Always use it in production.
steps.warning: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction block.

steps.title: Check index usage statistics
steps.description: Verify that your indexes are actually being used by queries.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "SELECT indexrelname, idx_scan, idx_tup_read, idx_tup_fetch FROM pg_stat_user_indexes WHERE schemaname = 'public' ORDER BY idx_scan DESC LIMIT 10;"

steps.title: Find unused indexes wasting space
steps.description: Identify indexes that consume disk space but are never used by the query planner.
steps.command: psql -U postgres -d mydb -c "SELECT indexrelname, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size, idx_scan FROM pg_stat_user_indexes WHERE idx_scan = 0 AND indexrelname NOT LIKE '%pkey%' ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC;"

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# PostgreSQL Performance Tuning
set -euo pipefail

DB="mydb"
USER="postgres"

echo "=== Tables with high sequential scans ==="
psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -c "
SELECT relname AS table,
       seq_scan AS seq_scans,
       seq_tup_read AS rows_seq_read,
       idx_scan AS idx_scans,
       n_live_tup AS live_rows
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE seq_scan > 50
ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC
LIMIT 10;
"

echo ""
echo "=== Unused indexes ==="
psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -c "
SELECT indexrelname AS index_name,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size,
       idx_scan AS times_used
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE idx_scan = 0
  AND indexrelname NOT LIKE '%pkey%'
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(indexrelid) DESC
LIMIT 10;
"

echo ""
echo "=== Most used indexes ==="
psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -c "
SELECT indexrelname AS index_name,
       idx_scan AS times_used,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC
LIMIT 10;
"

echo ""
echo "=== Cache hit ratio ==="
psql -U "$USER" -d "$DB" -c "
SELECT round(100.0 * sum(blks_hit) / nullif(sum(blks_hit) + sum(blks_read), 0), 2) AS cache_hit_ratio
FROM pg_stat_database
WHERE datname = '$DB';
"

faq.question: What is a good cache hit ratio?
faq.answer: Aim for 99%+ cache hit ratio in production. Below 95% suggests your shared_buffers or effective_cache_size settings are too low, or your working set exceeds available memory.

faq.question: Should I index every column used in WHERE clauses?
faq.answer: No. Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes and consume disk space. Focus on columns used in frequently executed queries with high selectivity. Use composite indexes for multi-column WHERE clauses.

faq.question: What does CONCURRENTLY do in CREATE INDEX?
faq.answer: It builds the index without holding a write lock on the table, allowing INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE to continue during index creation. It takes longer but avoids blocking production traffic.
